Gladiator 2 is grossly overrated—frankly, it’s bad. The script is poorly written, the dialogues are uninspired, and the story feels contrived. The idea of making Lucius the son of Maximus is nonsensical and reeks of desperation to force Maximus’ legacy into the sequel. The narrative is clunky and lacks the seamless flow of the original. Worse still, the fantastical addition of fighting sharks in the Colosseum is absurd and undermines the grounded realism that made the first film great. To top it off, the actor playing Lucius is far less charismatic than Russell Crowe, leaving the sequel feeling hollow. The director should have taken a more original approach instead of lazily attempting to replicate the magic of the first movie.

For the people hating on gladiator 2 and having a 78 percent on rotten tomatoes:

Gladiator II is not expected to be a flop, with critics giving it mostly positive reviews and a projected strong opening weekend: 
Reviews
Critics have given Gladiator II mostly positive reviews, with a 77% freshness rating on Rotten Tomatoes and a 67 average on Metacritic. Some critics have called it "gobsmacking" and "loopy". 
Opening weekend
Box Office Theory projects that Gladiator II will have a strong opening weekend, with a low-end projection of $53 million and a high-end projection of $75 million. This is more than the opening weekend of the original Gladiator in 2000, which was $34.8 million. 
Budget
The film's budget was originally $165 million, but ended up being closer to $310 million. 
Gladiator II is set years after the events of the original Gladiator and stars Paul Mescal, Denzel Washington, and Pedro Pascal. It tells the story of Lucius Verus, the son of Maximus, who is forced to fight in the Colosseum after Rome's emperors conquer his home.

Gladiator worked profoundly well b/c of its story. An amazing story that hit all the structural plot points with such amazing flair. 

Here's one: the Midpoint, though less important than Act 1's Inciting Incident (when Marcus Aurelius requests that Maximus be his daughter's & grandson's guardian after his death, to which he reluctantly agrees), or Act 3's Crisis Decision/Climax (when Maximus suddenly realizes Commodus offers to fight him in the arena, & he decides this will be how he avenges his wife's & son's murder & honors his promise to Marcus, by killing Commodus out on the arena sand). 

Unlike those seminal plot points the Act 2 Midpoint is when Commodus, down in the arena, demands that this mystery gladiator turn & face him & reveal his identity. Maximus turns & removes his mask & declares who he is & what he WILL do. "I am Maximus Decimus Meridius, leader of the Felix Legion, husband to a murdered wife, father of a murdered son, & loyal vassal of the true Emperor, Marcus Aurelius. And I WILL have my vengeance, in this life or the next!" This is the strongest Midpoint I've ever seen in 50 years of film viewing. The hero's identity is revealed to the story's antagonist, & declares his allegiance, what was done to him & his family, & what his unequivocal intention is. It's the dead-center anchor of the story & of Maximus' tragic journey. 

I hope Gladiator II's story echoes the supreme structural punch that the first film had. That's what blew audiences away, it was operatic grand cinema at its best!

For all those wondering - Everyone is hating because this ruins the legacy of the first one. Gladiator was an absolute masterpiece because of it's authenticity and originality at the time it released. It was never meant to have a sequel. The last thing anyone thought after watching Gladiator was "when is the sequel coming?" 

This does not seem like an attempt by Ridley Scott to try recapturing the inspirational fire that he had in the late 90s and early 2000s. It feels more like he's making one last attempt at creating a blockbuster to pad his wallet by capitalizing on the work that won him best picture. And perhaps he's a little bitter that he did not win best director for Gladiator, so he feels his wallet needs compensation for the award he was deprived of. This is only my speculation.

The only redeeming reason this movie can possibly exist is for Ridley Scott to try modernizing the original and bring the story to a new generation of audience. Unfortunately much of the authenticity of the original will be lost with this one because of the the ludicrous use of CGI. Gladiator mostly used practical effects for it's action sequences, with a few sparing uses of CGI when necessary. And while the original is of course not totally historically faithful with its depiction of the Roman Empire, it is a much more realistic and historically accurate attempt at bringing the feel of ancient Rome to a modern audience. This trailer has much less tasteful use of CGI and clearly looks like no real effort was put into creating a historically accurate depiction of ancient Rome.

Of course you can't judge book by its cover. But based on the selection of shots this trailer uses to preview this movie, my expectations are incredibly low. They are so low that it essentially confirms a sequel to Gladiator never should have been considered in the first place. I will not be watching this movie unless there it universal acclaim from critics and audiences alike. It seems incredibly unlikely it will. I will choose to remember Gladiator as the masterpiece that it is without tainting its legacy by watching this.

Don't get me wrong, Lucilla was obviously madly in love with him, and it mysterious that all we know is that Lucius's dad is dead, so I could see how it could have been a case of her hiding the truth because he's now a married man with a family and she was the daughter of the Emperor so claiming that the father is dead would be less scandalous. Then again, Rome was the epitome of scandalous so that doesn't stick... I don't know. Just watch the original tonight and I can't see why a sequel was needed. It had been hinted at in the first movie that Lucius was born of an affair. Maximus' last words were "Lucius is safe?". And both Lucius and his deceased son were around 8, and we know Lucilla and Maximus had a thing, and hadn't seen each other for a long time since the Roman empires victory in Germania. Lucius Verus, Lucilla's husband was the previous Emperor of Rome. It would be a huge scandal for her to have a child born of an affair, against the previous Emperor, from a general who wasn't even a Roman. As a general, a Spaniard one at that, he's not even allowed to enter Rome without handing over his title as General, which is partly why it was such a huge deal that Marcus Aurelius gave up the role of Caesar to Maximus, who is considered an outsider to the Roman's in high society.

I wish they would have created an opportunity for Maximus to know for sure - not that he needed any more motivation to overcome Commodus, but just to make it that much more weighty. He couldn't save his wife and son, but he ends up saving his other son. I don't know - perhaps this new information would introduce too much complexity and backfire, since one of the things that makes Gladiator such an epic classic is its simple plot.
